homas ptides ptolemy ptpresenters thales triq System-level Synthesis of Dataflow Applications for FPGA-based Distributed Platforms Hugo Andrade , Kaushik Ravindran , Alejandro Asenjo, Casey Weltzin Citation Hugo Andrade, Kaushik Ravindran, Alejandro

tation Hugo Andrade, Kaushik Ravindran, Alejandro Asenjo, Casey Weltzin. "System-level Synthesis of Dataflow Applications for FPGA-based Distributed Platforms". Talk or presentation, 7, November, 2013; Presented at the 10th Biennial Ptolemy Miniconf

3; Presented at the 10th Biennial Ptolemy Miniconference , Berkeley. Abstract With the increasing popularity of FPGAs (due to their competitive and deployable performance and price), system-level synthesis of applications onto FPGA-based platforms is

hesis of applications onto FPGA-based platforms is a key focus area for system-design tool and research communities. In particular, developing efficient and effective support for implementing advanced dataflow applications (such as next-generation co

dataflow applications (such as next-generation communication systems, complex cyber-physical systems, or high performance scientific computations) by domain experts (with little or no knowledge of FPGA hardware and/or integration of I/O) is an impor

GA hardware and/or integration of I/O) is an important problem. In this talk we study the relevance of input language and model of computation in capturing user intent and their roles in increasing productivity for system designers, and summarize sta

oductivity for system designers, and summarize state-of-the-art techniques for implementing dataflow applications on FPGA-based distributed platforms. The presentation will focus on three topics: - (a) synthesis of domain-centric algorithms (targ

- (a) synthesis of domain-centric algorithms (targeted for a single FPGA) while optimizing for constraints like performance and area using directives (also called high-level synthesis or HLS); - (b) application design using domain specific models of

application design using domain specific models of computation (which formally captures design intent), and associated analysis of key properties and subsequent implementation on a single FPGA; and - (c) synthesis of system level applications for mu

(c) synthesis of system level applications for multi-FPGA platforms; the last step integrates techniques for (a) and (b), and additionally solves mapping and synchronization of distributed computation and communication. We conclude by discussing a m

n and communication. We conclude by discussing a more general problem of implementing system-level applications over heterogeneous multi-target platforms that include traditional instruction processors, FPGAs, GPUs, specialized processors, and accele

s, FPGAs, GPUs, specialized processors, and accelerators. Along the way, we also highlight related research efforts at National Instruments R&D in these areas. Electronic downloads Andrade_System-level_synthesis_of_dataflow_applications_for_FPGA-base
